schools.summary: Gill: thou shalt not raise a false report of a neighbour or any man, by private slanders, whispers, backbiting, and tale-bearing, nor join hands with the wicked to bear false witness; the witnesses note God forbidding to raise a false report or to join the wicked as an unrighteous witness — the guarding of truth and justice against slander and false testimony (God forbidding both the spreading and the supporting of a lie: 'Thou shalt not raise a false report: put not thine hand with the wicked to be an unrighteous witness'; the first part, 'Thou shalt not raise a false report,' that no man should start or spread a false and slanderous tale of his neighbour, whether by private slanders, whispers, backbiting, and tale-bearing; the second part, 'put not thine hand with the wicked to be an unrighteous witness,' that none should join with the wicked to bear false witness in a court, becoming a party to injustice; that this guards the ninth commandment, forbidding both the origin and the support of falsehood that wrongs another; that to abet a lie is to share its guilt, and that the receiver of a false report is as bad as the raiser; that God, the God of truth, hates all falsehood).

themes: Thou shalt not raise a false report
put not thine hand with the wicked to be an unrighteous witness
God forbidding both the raising and the supporting of a false report
to abet a lie is to share its guilt, for God, the God of truth, hates all falsehood

schools.summary: Gill: the examples of the many, who are generally the worst, are not to be followed in evil; the Targums add, but to do good; the witnesses note God forbidding to follow a multitude to do evil, or to speak in a cause so as to wrest judgment after the many — the warning against the corrupting power of the crowd and against perverting justice (God forbidding the following of the many in evil: 'Thou shalt not follow a multitude to do evil; neither shalt thou speak in a cause to decline after many to wrest judgment'; the first charge, 'Thou shalt not follow a multitude to do evil,' that the example of the many, who are generally the worst, is not to be followed into sin, the crowd being no safe guide; the addition the Targums make, 'but to do good,' that the many should be followed only in what is right; the second charge, against the judge or witness, 'neither shalt thou speak in a cause to decline after many to wrest judgment,' that none should, in giving testimony or judgment, side with the majority to pervert justice against the right; the great lesson, that the multitude is not to be followed in evil, nor numbers mistaken for truth, but conscience and justice held against the crowd; that the broad way is the way of the many and is not to be trodden).

debates.question: What does 'Thou shalt not follow a multitude to do evil' teach?

sides.position: That the crowd is no safe guide — the many being generally the worst; numbers are not to be mistaken for truth, nor the majority sided with to pervert justice; conscience and justice are to be held against the crowd, for the broad way is the way of the many

themes: Thou shalt not follow a multitude to do evil
neither shalt thou speak in a cause to decline after many to wrest judgment
God forbidding to follow the crowd into evil or to side with the majority to wrest judgment
numbers not to be mistaken for truth, conscience and justice held against the crowd

schools.summary: Gill: thou shalt not favour a poor man and endeavour to carry his cause for him, right or wrong, from a foolish pity; justice is to be impartial; the witnesses note God forbidding to countenance a poor man in his cause merely because he is poor — the impartiality of justice, not to be swayed even by pity (justice must be blind to the person, even the poor: 'Neither shalt thou countenance a poor man in his cause'; the surprising charge, that even a poor man is not to be countenanced or favoured in his cause, right or wrong, from a foolish pity; that, while the rich are not to be favoured for their wealth, the more common danger, neither are the poor to be favoured for their poverty, lest justice be perverted on the other side; the great principle, that justice is to be impartial, regarding only the right of the cause and not the person, neither the rich for their bribes nor the poor for their misery; that even a good feeling, like pity, must not be allowed to pervert judgment, for to wrong the rich in favour of the poor is still to do injustice; that God's justice is no respecter of persons).

themes: Neither shalt thou countenance
a poor man in his cause
God forbidding to favour even the poor in their cause, justice impartial
regarding only the right, not the person; even pity must not pervert judgment

schools.summary: Gill: if thou meet thine enemy's ox or ass going astray, or any other beast, thou shalt bring it back; these are mentioned as common instances; the witnesses note God commanding that, if one meet his enemy's ox or ass going astray, he should surely bring it back — the duty of doing good even to an enemy, the law of love over private grudge (even an enemy's loss to be cared for: 'If thou meet thine enemy's ox or his ass going astray, thou shalt surely bring it back to him again'; the case, that one might meet thine enemy's ox or his ass going astray, lost and wandering; the duty, 'thou shalt surely bring it back to him again,' that, far from leaving it to perish or seizing it for himself, or rejoicing in his enemy's loss, he should restore the straying beast to its owner, though that owner be his enemy; the great principle the commentators draw, that the law requires kindness and good even to an enemy, overcoming private grudge and personal hatred by active goodness; that this looks on to Christ's word, Love your enemies, do good to them that hate you; that true religion teaches us to seek even our enemy's good).

themes: If thou meet thine enemy's ox or his ass going astray
thou shalt surely bring it back to him again
the duty of doing good even to an enemy, restoring his straying beast
overcoming private grudge by active goodness, looking on to Christ's 'Love your enemies'

schools.summary: Gill: if thou see the ass of him that hateth thee fallen down under such a burden that it cannot rise, and the owner cannot raise it alone, thou shalt help; the witnesses note God commanding that, if one see the ass of him that hates him lying under its burden, he should not forbear but help — the duty of active help even to one who hates us, overcoming the temptation to pass by (we must help even the hater's beast in its distress: 'If thou see the ass of him that hateth thee lying under his burden, and wouldest forbear to help him, thou shalt surely help with him'; the case, that the beast of him that hateth thee is lying under his burden, fallen and unable to rise; the temptation owned, 'and wouldest forbear to help him,' that the natural inclination, seeing it is an enemy's, is to pass by and not help, even to be glad of his trouble; the command against that inclination, 'thou shalt surely help with him,' that he must overcome his reluctance and join with the hater to raise the fallen beast, doing him active good; the great lesson the commentators draw, that the law requires not only to do an enemy no harm but to do him positive good, even helping him in his distress, and so subduing our own resentment; that this overcoming of evil with good is the spirit of the gospel).

themes: If thou see the ass of him that hateth thee lying under his burden, and wouldest forbear to help him
thou shalt surely help with him
God commanding active help even to a hater's fallen beast
the law requiring not only no harm but positive good, the overcoming of evil with good

schools.summary: Gill: as the poor man was not to be favoured when his cause was bad, so he was not to be wronged when his cause was good; his judgment was not to be wrested; the witnesses note God forbidding to wrest the judgment of the poor in his cause — the protecting of the poor from injustice, the other side of impartial justice (the poor man's right not to be denied him: 'Thou shalt not wrest the judgment of thy poor in his cause'; the balance with the earlier law, that, as the poor was not to be favoured when his cause was bad, so neither was he to be wronged when his cause was good; the particular danger guarded, 'wrest the judgment of thy poor,' that the poor, having no money to bribe and no power to defend himself, was the most likely to have his right denied or perverted, and so God especially guards him; the equity of God's law, that it holds the balance even, neither favouring the poor against the right nor wronging him against it; that God is the special protector of the poor and helpless, and will avenge the wresting of their judgment; that to oppress the poor in his cause is a great sin in God's sight).

themes: Thou shalt not wrest the judgment of thy poor
in his cause
God forbidding to pervert the judgment of the poor, who cannot bribe nor defend himself
the balance of justice even, neither favouring the poor when wrong nor wronging him when right

schools.summary: Gill: keep thee far from a false matter, or word, from receiving a false testimony, or taking the wrong side of a cause, or engaging in a bad one, keep aloof from it; the witnesses note God charging to keep far from a false matter, not to slay the innocent and righteous, for he will not justify the wicked — the abhorrence of falsehood and the guarding of innocent blood (God would have his people stand wholly clear of every false cause: 'Keep thee far from a false matter; and the innocent and righteous slay thou not: for I will not justify the wicked'; the breadth of the charge, 'Keep thee far from a false matter,' that they should not merely avoid lying but keep far off, aloof, from every false cause, false testimony, or wrong side, not so much as touching it; the gravest case, 'the innocent and righteous slay thou not,' that they should never, by false witness or unjust judgment, take away the life of the innocent, the shedding of innocent blood being a most heinous sin; the solemn reason, 'for I will not justify the wicked,' that God, the righteous Judge, will not acquit the guilty who do such things but will surely punish them, however they may escape men's courts; that God sees and will avenge the wrong done to the innocent; that the fear of God's judgment should keep men from all false dealing).

themes: Keep thee far from a false matter; and the innocent and righteous slay thou not
for I will not justify the wicked
God charging to keep wholly clear of every false matter and never to slay the innocent
God the righteous Judge will not justify the wicked, but will avenge the innocent

schools.summary: Gill: thou shalt take no gift of the persons whose cause is tried, neither of one side nor the other, neither before the trial nor after, not even to judge truly; the witnesses note God forbidding the judge to take any gift, for the gift blinds the wise and perverts the words of the righteous — the guarding of justice against the corrupting power of the bribe (no gift may be taken in judgment: 'thou shalt take no gift: for the gift blindeth the wise, and perverteth the words of the righteous'; the strictness, 'thou shalt take no gift,' that the judge should take nothing of the persons whose cause is tried, from either side, before or after the trial, not even to judge truly, lest his judgment be swayed; the reason, 'for the gift blindeth the wise,' that even a wise man's eyes are darkened by a bribe, so that he cannot see the right clearly; the further harm, 'and perverteth the words of the righteous,' that even a righteous man, taking a gift, is led to twist his words and pervert his sentence; the great danger of bribery, that it corrupts the best, blinding the wise and perverting the just; that justice must be kept clean of all corrupt influence).

themes: And thou shalt take no gift
for the gift blindeth the wise, and perverteth the words of the righteous
God forbidding the judge to take any gift or bribe
the bribe corrupting the best, blinding the wise and perverting the righteous

schools.summary: Gill: the stranger was not to be vexed and oppressed in private nor in a public court of judicature, by judges not doing them justice or showing partiality against them; the witnesses note God forbidding to oppress a stranger, for they knew the heart of a stranger, having themselves been strangers in Egypt — the protecting of the foreigner, with the appeal to their own remembered suffering (God guarding the stranger by their own experience: 'thou shalt not oppress a stranger: for ye know the heart of a stranger, seeing ye were strangers in the land of Egypt'; the protection, 'thou shalt not oppress a stranger,' that the foreigner, having no kindred or power in the land, was not to be vexed and oppressed, neither privately nor in the courts, but to have justice; the tender and searching reason, 'for ye know the heart of a stranger,' that they, of all people, knew by experience what a stranger feels, the loneliness, the fear, the temptation to despair; the ground, 'seeing ye were strangers in the land of Egypt,' that, having been strangers and oppressed in Egypt, they should the more pity and not oppress the strangers among them; the great lesson the commentators draw, that our own experience of suffering should make us tender to others in like case; that God remembers and pleads the cause of the friendless stranger).

themes: thou shalt not oppress a stranger; for ye know the heart of a stranger
seeing ye were strangers in the land of Egypt
God forbidding the oppression of the stranger by their own remembered suffering
our own experience of suffering should make us tender to others, God pleading the cause of the friendless

schools.summary: Gill: six years thou shalt sow thy land, the land of Canaan given to them, which they were to till and gather its fruits; the witnesses note God appointing that for six years they should sow their land and gather its fruits — the granting of six years' tillage before the land's rest (God marking off the six years of labour for the land: 'six years thou shalt sow thy land, and shalt gather in the fruits thereof'; the grant, 'six years thou shalt sow thy land,' that for six years together they should till, plant, and sow the land of Canaan, their inheritance; the harvest allowed, 'and shalt gather in the fruits thereof,' that they should reap and gather in the produce of those six years, freely enjoying the fruit of their labour; that this lays the ground for the law of the seventh year's rest, as the six working days lay the ground for the sabbath; the goodness of God, who gives ample time for the land's tillage and the gathering of its fruits; that the same God who appointed the sabbath of days appointed also a sabbath of years).

themes: And six years thou shalt sow thy land
and shalt gather in the fruits thereof
God granting six years for tilling and gathering the fruits of the land
as the six working days lay the ground for the sabbath, so the six years for the sabbath of years

schools.summary: Gill: 'let it rest' from perfect tillage, ploughing and sowing, 'and lie still' from dunging and harrowing, the fruits made common; the witnesses note God appointing that in the seventh year the land should rest, its fruits left for the poor and the beasts, and so also the vineyard and oliveyard — the sabbatical year, a rest for the land and a provision for the poor (the land itself keeping a sabbath: 'the seventh year thou shalt let it rest and lie still; that the poor of thy people may eat... In like manner thou shalt deal with thy vineyard, and with thy oliveyard'; the rest of the land, 'thou shalt let it rest and lie still,' that in the seventh year the land was to lie fallow, free from ploughing and sowing, dunging and harrowing, at rest from tillage; the gracious purpose, 'that the poor of thy people may eat,' that what grew of itself that year was made common, that the poor, who had no land, might freely gather and eat; the further mercy, 'and what they leave the beasts of the field shall eat,' that even the wild beasts should have their share; the extension, 'in like manner... with thy vineyard, and with thy oliveyard,' the same rest and common use of the vines and olives; the lessons the commentators draw, the trust in God's provision, who would bless the sixth year to supply three, the care for the poor, and the land's own sabbath).

themes: the seventh year thou shalt let it rest and lie still; that the poor of thy people may eat
In like manner thou shalt deal with thy vineyard, and with thy oliveyard
the sabbatical year, the land lying fallow, its fruits made common for the poor and the beasts
teaching trust in God's provision, care for the poor, and the land's own sabbath

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: that, though bound to keep the sabbatical year, they must not neglect the seventh day's rest or weekly Sabbath, which was of perpetual obligation; the witnesses note God appointing six days for work and the seventh for rest, that ox, ass, servant, and stranger might be refreshed — the weekly sabbath of mercy, rest for the labouring and the lowly (the seventh day bringing rest to all who toil: 'Six days thou shalt do thy work, and on the seventh day thou shalt rest: that thine ox and thine ass may rest, and the son of thy handmaid, and the stranger, may be refreshed'; that the weekly sabbath, though distinct from the sabbatical year, is here pressed as of perpetual obligation, not to be neglected; the rest granted, 'on the seventh day thou shalt rest,' the weekly cessation from labour; the merciful purpose toward the lowly and the beasts, 'that thine ox and thine ass may rest, and the son of thy handmaid, and the stranger, may be refreshed,' that the sabbath was a kindness to the labouring beasts, the bondservant, and the stranger, that they too might rest and be refreshed; the goodness of God in the sabbath, providing rest for the weary, even the lowest and the beasts; that God's law cares for the toiling creature).

themes: Six days thou shalt do thy work, and on the seventh day thou shalt rest
that thine ox and thine ass may rest, and the son of thy handmaid, and the stranger, may be refreshed
the weekly sabbath of mercy, of perpetual obligation, rest for the labouring and lowly
the goodness of God providing rest for the weary, even the lowest and the toiling beasts

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'be circumspect,' rather 'take heed'; make no mention of the name of other gods, the verb meaning even to swear by them or invoke them; Gill notes be careful to keep all the commands punctually and constantly; the witnesses note God charging them to be circumspect in all he had said, and to make no mention of the name of other gods, nor let it be heard in their mouth — the call to careful obedience and to utter separation from idolatry (God summing the laws with a charge of heed and of separation: 'in all things that I have said unto you be circumspect: and make no mention of the name of other gods, neither let it be heard out of thy mouth'; the general charge, 'in all things... be circumspect,' or take heed, that they should be careful to keep all the commands punctually and constantly, watching over their obedience; the special charge against idolatry, 'make no mention of the name of other gods,' that they should not so much as name the heathen gods in a way of honour, nor swear by them, nor invoke them; the strictness, 'neither let it be heard out of thy mouth,' that the very names of the idols should not pass their lips with reverence, so utterly were they to abhor and renounce idolatry; the great principle the commentators draw, that God's people are to keep themselves wholly separate from idolatry, in word as in deed; that careful obedience and entire separation from false worship are required).

themes: in all things that I have said unto you be circumspect
make no mention of the name of other gods, neither let it be heard out of thy mouth
the charge of careful obedience and utter separation from idolatry
God's people to keep wholly separate from idolatry, in word as in deed, not even naming the idols

schools.summary: Gill: the feast of the passover, the feast of weeks or pentecost fifty days after, and the feast of tabernacles on the fifteenth of Tisri; the witnesses note God commanding three feasts to be kept to him in the year — the appointing of the three great annual solemnities (God fixing three set times of festal worship: 'Three times thou shalt keep a feast unto me in the year'; the number, 'three times... in the year,' that there should be three great feasts yearly, at which the people should come together to worship; which they were, the feast of the passover with unleavened bread in the spring, the feast of weeks or pentecost fifty days after, and the feast of tabernacles or ingathering in the autumn; that these were feasts unto me, kept to the LORD, times of joy, worship, and remembrance of his mercies; the type the commentators draw, that the three feasts shadow forth the great works of redemption, the passover, Christ our passover sacrificed, pentecost, the firstfruits and the gift of the Spirit, and tabernacles, the final ingathering and rest; that God appoints set times for his people to rejoice before him).

debates.question: What do the three annual feasts shadow forth?

sides.position: The great works of redemption — the passover, Christ our passover sacrificed; pentecost (the feast of harvest/firstfruits), the firstfruits and the gift of the Spirit; and tabernacles (the feast of ingathering), the final ingathering and rest

themes: Three times thou shalt keep a feast unto me
in the year
God appointing three great yearly feasts kept to the LORD
the feasts shadowing the works of redemption: Christ our passover, the Spirit, and the final ingathering

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the feast of unleavened bread, in the time appointed of the month Abib, for in it they came out from Egypt; the witnesses note God appointing the feast of unleavened bread in the month Abib, the time of their coming out of Egypt, and that none should appear before him empty — the first feast, the memorial of the deliverance, kept with a gift (the passover-feast remembering the redemption: 'Thou shalt keep the feast of unleavened bread... in the time appointed of the month Abib; for in it thou camest out from Egypt: and none shall appear before me empty'; the first of the three feasts, 'the feast of unleavened bread,' kept seven days in the month Abib, when no leavened bread was to be eaten; the ground, 'for in it thou camest out from Egypt,' that the feast was the memorial of the great deliverance, kept in the very month it was wrought; the charge, 'none shall appear before me empty,' that none should come to the feast without a gift or offering, but each bring some present according to his ability, owning God's mercies; the lesson the commentators draw, that we should not come before God empty but bring him our thank-offerings, and chiefly the offering of ourselves; that the redeemed should ever remember their redemption with thankful worship).

themes: Thou shalt keep the feast of unleavened bread... for in it thou camest out from Egypt
and none shall appear before me empty
the feast of unleavened bread, the memorial of the deliverance, kept with a gift
the lesson that we should not come before God empty, but bring our thank-offerings, and chiefly ourselves

schools.summary: Gill: the feast of harvest, the feast of wheat harvest, sometimes called the feast of weeks, at which the firstfruits were brought, and the feast of ingathering at the year's end; the witnesses note God appointing the feast of harvest, with its firstfruits, and the feast of ingathering at the year's end — the second and third feasts, of the firstfruits and of the final harvest (God appointing feasts for the first and the last of the harvest: 'the feast of harvest, the firstfruits of thy labours... and the feast of ingathering, which is in the end of the year, when thou hast gathered in thy labours'; the second feast, 'the feast of harvest, the firstfruits of thy labours,' pentecost or the feast of weeks, when the firstfruits of the wheat harvest were brought to God, owning him the giver of the increase; the third feast, 'the feast of ingathering, which is in the end of the year,' tabernacles, when, the whole harvest gathered in, they kept a feast of thanksgiving for all the fruits of the field; the fitness, that the seasons of the harvest, the beginning and the full gathering, were marked by feasts of joy and thanks to God; the type the commentators draw, of the firstfruits of the Spirit, pentecost, and the great ingathering of the final harvest, tabernacles; that God would have his people thank him for the fruits of the earth).

themes: the feast of harvest, the firstfruits of thy labours, which thou hast sown in the field
and the feast of ingathering, which is in the end of the year
the feasts of firstfruits (pentecost) and of ingathering (tabernacles), feasts of thanks
a type of the firstfruits of the Spirit and the great ingathering of the final harvest

schools.summary: Gill: in the city of Jerusalem, when they were come into the land and the temple was built, there they were to show themselves before the Lord as being his and devoted to his service; the witnesses note God requiring that all the males appear before the Lord GOD three times in the year — the gathering of the males to the place of worship at the three feasts (the men of Israel presenting themselves before God: 'Three times in the year all thy males shall appear before the Lord GOD'; the duty, 'all thy males shall appear before the Lord GOD,' that, at each of the three feasts, the men of Israel were to come up to the place God should choose, afterward Jerusalem, to present themselves before him; the meaning, that they came to show themselves before the Lord as being his and devoted to his service, owning their allegiance and worshipping him together; the wonder of God's care the commentators mark, that, while all the males were thus drawn away from their homes three times a year, God promised that none should desire their land, keeping their borders safe; that God would have his people gather for solemn united worship; the gospel pattern, that believers should not forsake the assembling of themselves together).

themes: Three times in the year
all thy males shall appear before the Lord GOD
all the males to present themselves before God at the three feasts
gathering for united worship, God keeping their borders safe while they were away

schools.summary: Gill: this belongs to the feast of the passover, the sacrifice of the passover, when the paschal lamb was killed, its blood shed, and its flesh eaten with unleavened bread; the witnesses note God forbidding to offer the blood of his sacrifice with leavened bread, or to let the fat remain till morning — the guarding of the passover offering against leaven and corruption (God ordering his sacrifice be kept pure and fresh: 'Thou shalt not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leavened bread; neither shall the fat of my sacrifice remain until the morning'; the first rule, 'not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leavened bread,' that the passover lamb, whose blood was shed, should be eaten with unleavened, not leavened, bread, leaven being the type of sin and corruption, to be put away; the second rule, 'neither shall the fat of my sacrifice remain until the morning,' that the fat or the flesh of the sacrifice should be wholly consumed that night, not kept till morning, lest it grow stale and corrupt; the lessons the commentators draw, that God's worship must be pure, free from the leaven of sin, and offered freshly and wholly, nothing of it kept back or left to corrupt; the type, that Christ our passover is to be fed on with sincerity and truth, the leaven of malice purged out).

themes: Thou shalt not offer the blood of my sacrifice with leavened bread
neither shall the fat of my sacrifice remain until the morning
the passover offering kept pure (no leaven, the type of sin) and fresh (nothing till morning)
a type that Christ our passover is fed on with sincerity and truth, the leaven of malice purged out

schools.summary: Gill: the first of the firstfruits of the land, of barley, wheat, wine, and oil, were to be brought, and the kid not seethed in its mother's milk; the witnesses note God commanding the firstfruits to be brought to his house, and forbidding to seethe a kid in its mother's milk — the offering of the first and best to God, and a law of mercy and separation from heathen rites (God claiming the firstfruits and guarding even against cruelty: 'The first of the firstfruits of thy land thou shalt bring into the house of the LORD thy God. Thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk'; the firstfruits, 'the first of the firstfruits of thy land thou shalt bring into the house of the LORD,' that the first and best of the produce, of corn, wine, and oil, should be given to God, owning him the giver of all and consecrating the whole; the second precept, 'Thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk,' variously taken, some as a law of mercy and natural tenderness, that it was an unkind and unnatural thing to boil the young in the very milk that nourished it, others, with many, as a forbidding of a heathen rite or superstition, the Canaanites so seething a kid in a magical custom of their idolatry, from which Israel was to keep separate; the lessons drawn, the giving of the first and best to God, tenderness even to the beasts, and separation from heathen ways).

debates.question: Why is it forbidden to 'seethe a kid in his mother's milk'?

sides.position: A law of mercy and natural tenderness — it being unkind and unnatural to boil the young in the very milk that nourished it
A forbidding of a heathen idolatrous rite — the Canaanites so seething a kid in a magical custom of their idolatry, from which Israel was to keep separate

themes: The first of the firstfruits of thy land thou shalt bring into the house of the LORD thy God
Thou shalt not seethe a kid in his mother's milk
God claiming the first and best of the produce as firstfruits
the kid-law taken as mercy and tenderness, or as separation from a heathen idolatrous rite

schools.summary: Gill: not a created angel, but the uncreated one, the Angel of God's presence, who was with the Israelites at Sinai and in the wilderness, who saved, redeemed, bore, and carried them; the witnesses note God promising to send his Angel before Israel, to keep them in the way and bring them to the place prepared — the great promise of the divine Angel, the uncreated Angel of God's presence, to lead his people (God giving them a divine Guide: 'Behold, I send an angel before thee, to keep thee in the way, and to bring thee into the place which I have prepared'; who this Angel is, that he is not a created angel but the uncreated one, the Angel of God's presence, the same who was with them at the sea and Sinai, a pre-incarnate appearing of Christ, the divine Guide of his people; the office, 'to keep thee in the way, and to bring thee into the place which I have prepared,' that the Angel should guard them on their journey and bring them safely into Canaan, the land God had prepared for them; the comfort, that God's people are led and kept by a divine Guide, who brings them at last to the place prepared; that Christ goes before his people to lead them to their inheritance).

debates.question: Who is the Angel God sends before Israel?

sides.position: Not a created angel, but the uncreated Angel of God's presence — a pre-incarnate appearing of Christ — who was with them at the sea and Sinai, the divine Guide of his people, a type of Christ leading them to their inheritance

themes: Behold, I send an angel before thee, to keep thee in the way
and to bring thee into the place which I have prepared
God sending the uncreated Angel of his presence to keep and guide Israel
a pre-incarnate appearing of Christ, the divine Guide bringing his people to their inheritance

schools.summary: Gill: beware of his face or countenance, observe his looks and his directions, laws and commands; the witnesses note God charging them to beware of the Angel, obey his voice, and not provoke him, for he would not pardon their transgressions, his name being in him — the divine authority of the Angel, who pardons or punishes, for God's own name is in him (the Angel owned as God himself: 'Beware of him, and obey his voice, provoke him not; for he will not pardon your transgressions: for my name is in him'; the reverence required, 'Beware of him, and obey his voice,' that they should heed his looks and obey his commands, walking carefully before him; the warning, 'provoke him not; for he will not pardon your transgressions,' that to provoke and rebel against him would not be passed over, for he had power to punish; the great reason, 'for my name is in him,' that the Angel bears God's own name and nature, is one with God, having divine authority to pardon or to punish; the strong proof of the Angel's deity the commentators draw, that my name is in him, for to forgive sins is God's prerogative, and the Angel, who pardons or refuses to pardon, must be God himself, the divine Son; that Christ, in whom the Father's name and nature dwell, is to be obeyed and not provoked).

debates.question: What does 'for my name is in him' prove about the Angel?

sides.position: His deity — for to pardon or refuse to pardon transgressions is God's prerogative; the Angel bearing God's own name and nature, one with God, must be God himself, the divine Son in whom the Father's name dwells, to be feared and obeyed

themes: Beware of him, and obey his voice, provoke him not; for he will not pardon your transgressions
for my name is in him
the divine authority of the Angel, who pardons or punishes
a proof of his deity, the divine Son in whom God's name and nature dwell, to be feared and obeyed

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'an adversary unto thine adversaries,' God would set himself against all who set themselves against Israel; the witnesses note God promising that, if they indeed obeyed his voice and did all he spoke, he would be an enemy to their enemies and an adversary to their adversaries — the great promise of God's protection on the condition of obedience (God making their cause his own: 'if thou shalt indeed obey his voice, and do all that I speak; then I will be an enemy unto thine enemies, and an adversary unto thine adversaries'; the condition, 'if thou shalt indeed obey his voice, and do all that I speak,' that the promise hangs on full and hearty obedience, hearkening hearken, diligently and constantly; the great promise, 'then I will be an enemy unto thine enemies, and an adversary unto thine adversaries,' that God would make their cause his own, opposing all who opposed them, fighting against their foes; the comfort the commentators draw, that, while they kept close to God, all the power of God was engaged on their side against their enemies; that to have God for our friend is to have him against all our foes, and that obedience is the way to this blessing; that God's people, obeying him, need fear no enemy).

themes: if thou shalt indeed obey his voice, and do all that I speak; then I will be an enemy unto thine enemies
and an adversary unto thine adversaries
God promising, on the condition of obedience, to make their cause his own
all God's power engaged on their side, obedience the way to this blessing

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: there are six of the seven nations mentioned here, the Girgashites being omitted, though some versions add them; the witnesses note God promising that his Angel should go before Israel, bring them in among the nations of Canaan, and cut them off — the renewed promise of the Angel's leading and the conquest of the land (God assuring the overthrow of the nations: 'mine Angel shall go before thee, and bring thee in unto the Amorites, and the Hittites... and I will cut them off'; the leading, 'mine Angel shall go before thee,' the same divine Angel, going before to bring them safely in; the nations named, 'the Amorites, and the Hittites, and the Perizzites, and the Canaanites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites,' six or seven peoples of Canaan, the inhabitants God would dispossess; the promise, 'and I will cut them off,' that God would destroy these nations before Israel, that his people might possess the land; the justice in it, that these nations, ripe in their iniquity, were to be cut off, and the land given to Israel; that the same Angel who leads also conquers, going before his people to overthrow their foes).

themes: mine Angel shall go before thee, and bring thee in unto the Amorites, and the Hittites
and I will cut them off
the divine Angel going before to bring Israel in and cut off the nations
the same Angel who leads also conquering, the nations ripe in iniquity dispossessed

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'break down their images,' destroy the objects of their idolatrous worship, leaving no trace; the witnesses note God forbidding to bow to or serve the gods of Canaan, or do after their works, but to overthrow them and break their images — the charge to utter separation from and destruction of idolatry (God will have no mixture with the worship of the heathen: 'Thou shalt not bow down to their gods, nor serve them, nor do after their works: but thou shalt utterly overthrow them, and quite break down their images'; the threefold prohibition, 'not bow down to their gods, nor serve them, nor do after their works,' that Israel should give the idols no honour, no service, and not imitate the heathen's ways and rites; the positive charge, 'but thou shalt utterly overthrow them, and quite break down their images,' that they should not only avoid but destroy the idols, leaving no trace of the objects of idolatrous worship; the great principle the commentators draw, that God's people must keep wholly separate from idolatry and false worship, neither sharing in it nor sparing it, but utterly putting it away; that to tolerate idolatry is to be ensnared by it, and that the surest safety is its destruction; that God will have his worship pure and undivided).

themes: Thou shalt not bow down to their gods, nor serve them, nor do after their works
but thou shalt utterly overthrow them, and quite break down their images
God charging utter separation from and destruction of the idols of Canaan
God's people to keep wholly separate, neither sharing idolatry nor sparing it, for to tolerate it is to be ensnared

schools.summary: Gill: ye shall serve the Lord your God, and him only, who had brought them out of Egypt and done so many great and good things for them; the witnesses note God promising that, if they served him, he would bless their bread and water and take sickness from them — the rich promise of blessing on their food and health upon true worship (God rewarding the worship of himself alone with blessing: 'ye shall serve the LORD your God, and he shall bless thy bread, and thy water; and I will take sickness away from the midst of thee'; the duty, 'ye shall serve the LORD your God,' that they should worship and serve the LORD alone, who had so greatly delivered and blessed them; the promise of blessing on their food, 'he shall bless thy bread, and thy water,' that God would bless their plainest fare, their very bread and water, making it nourishing and sufficient, the blessing of God on their common provision; the promise of health, 'I will take sickness away from the midst of thee,' that God would keep disease from among them, granting health and soundness; the comfort the commentators draw, that God's blessing on the daily provision and health of his people flows from their faithful worship of him; that to serve God is the way to every needful blessing).

themes: ye shall serve the LORD your God, and he shall bless thy bread, and thy water
and I will take sickness away from the midst of thee
God promising to bless their very bread and water and take sickness away
the blessing on their common provision and health flowing from faithful worship

schools.summary: Gill: there shall be no abortions or miscarriages, nor sterility or barrenness, either among the Israelites or their cattle; the witnesses note God promising that nothing should cast its young nor be barren in the land, and that he would fulfil the number of their days — the promise of fruitfulness and of length of life upon their obedience (God adding the blessings of increase and long life: 'There shall nothing cast their young, nor be barren, in thy land: the number of thy days I will fulfil'; the promise of fruitfulness, 'nothing cast their young, nor be barren,' that there should be no abortions or miscarriages, nor sterility or barrenness, neither among the people nor their cattle, but a blessed increase of family and flock; the promise of long life, 'the number of thy days I will fulfil,' that God would grant them to live out their full term, none cut off untimely, but their days fulfilled; the comfort the commentators draw, that fruitfulness and length of days are among the blessings of God's covenant, given to his obedient people; that life, health, and increase are all in God's hand, to give to those who serve him; that God's blessing makes a people fruitful and long-lived).

themes: There shall nothing cast their young, nor be barren, in thy land
the number of thy days I will fulfil
God promising fruitfulness and length of life to his obedient people
no miscarriage or barrenness, their days fulfilled, all in God's hand

schools.summary: Gill: God would send his fear before them, what should cause fear among the nations of Canaan, either the hornets, or the fame of his mighty works; the witnesses note God promising to send his fear before Israel, to destroy the peoples and make their enemies turn their backs — the going before of a divine terror to scatter the foe (God conquering their enemies by fear before the battle: 'I will send my fear before thee, and will destroy all the people to whom thou shalt come, and I will make all thine enemies turn their backs unto thee'; the means, 'I will send my fear before thee,' that God would send a terror upon the nations of Canaan, what should cause fear among them, whether by the hornets or the report of his mighty works at the sea and in the wilderness; the effect, 'will destroy all the people... and I will make all thine enemies turn their backs,' that the foe, struck with dread, should be destroyed and put to flight, turning their backs before Israel; that God can conquer his people's enemies by fear, melting their courage before a blow is struck; the comfort, that God goes before his people to scatter their foes, that the battle is the LORD's).

themes: I will send my fear before thee, and will destroy all the people to whom thou shalt come
and I will make all thine enemies turn their backs unto thee
God sending his fear before Israel to destroy and rout the nations
conquering his people's enemies by fear before a blow is struck, the battle the LORD's

schools.summary: Gill: the hornets may be interpreted either figuratively, signifying the fear before which should fall on the Canaanites, or literally, the stings of which were so painful; the witnesses note God promising to send hornets before Israel, to drive out the Hivite, Canaanite, and Hittite — the strange and lowly means by which God would dispossess the nations (God using even hornets to drive out the foe: 'I will send hornets before thee, which shall drive out the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite, from before thee'; the means, 'I will send hornets before thee,' variously taken, some figuratively as a name for the fear which should fall on the Canaanites, driving them out by terror, others literally, that God would send actual hornets whose stings were so painful and unbearable that the people fled the land; the effect, 'which shall drive out the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite,' that the nations should be driven out before Israel by this means; the great lesson the commentators draw, that God can use the smallest and meanest of his creatures, a hornet, to overthrow the mightiest nations, that the glory might be his; that God's deliverances are often wrought by weak and unlikely means, that no flesh should glory).

debates.question: What are the 'hornets' God sends before Israel?

sides.position: Literal hornets — actual stinging insects, so painful and unbearable that the people fled the land
A figure for the terror and fear that should fall on the Canaanites, driving them out

themes: And I will send hornets before thee
which shall drive out the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite, from before thee
God driving out the nations by hornets, whether literal or a figure for terror
God using the smallest of his creatures to overthrow the mightiest, that no flesh should glory

schools.summary: Gill: this is observed beforehand, lest the Israelites should be discouraged and fear they should never be rid of them, and it was so ordered in Providence; the witnesses note God declaring he would not drive out the nations in one year, lest the land become desolate and the beasts multiply — the wise reason for the gradual conquest, the care that the land be not left waste (God would not clear the land too fast, for their own good: 'I will not drive them out from before thee in one year; lest the land become desolate, and the beast of the field multiply against thee'; the forewarning, told beforehand, lest the Israelites should be discouraged and fear they should never be rid of the nations, that the slowness was of God's ordering, not his failure; the wise reason, 'lest the land become desolate, and the beast of the field multiply against thee,' that, if the nations were all cut off at once, the land, being but thinly peopled by Israel at first, would lie waste, the cities fall to ruin, and the wild beasts multiply and overrun it; the providence and wisdom of God in this, ordering the time of the conquest for his people's good, that they might be able to keep and fill the land as they grew; the lesson the commentators draw, that God's slower workings are often wiser and kinder than our haste would have them).

themes: I will not drive them out from before thee in one year
lest the land become desolate, and the beast of the field multiply against thee
God's wise reason for the gradual conquest, lest the land lie waste
God's slower workings often wiser and kinder than our haste would have them

schools.summary: Gill: by little and little I will drive them out, the inhabitants of Canaan being left partly to keep up the cities, that they might not fall to ruin, until Israel increased; the witnesses note God promising to drive out the nations by little and little, until Israel was increased and inherited the land — the gradual dispossession matched to Israel's growth (God would drive out the foe by degrees, as his people grew to fill the land: 'By little and little I will drive them out from before thee, until thou be increased, and inherit the land'; the manner, 'By little and little I will drive them out,' that the dispossession should be gradual, not all at once, the nations driven out by degrees; the measure, 'until thou be increased, and inherit the land,' that the driving out should keep pace with Israel's increase, so that as the people grew they should take and fill the land left by the nations; the providence and wisdom in this, that God fitted the conquest to his people's growth, neither leaving the land waste nor giving them more than they could hold; the lesson the commentators draw, that God often gives his blessings and victories by degrees, as we are able to receive and keep them, growing his people up to their inheritance; that God's gradual dealings are full of wisdom and care).

themes: By little and little I will drive them out from before thee
until thou be increased, and inherit the land
the gradual dispossession matched to Israel's growth, as they could fill the land
God often giving his blessings and victories by degrees, as we are able to receive and keep them

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: the bounds set from the Red Sea to the sea of the Philistines, and from the desert to the river Euphrates; the witnesses note God promising to set Israel's bounds from the Red Sea to the Mediterranean, and from the desert to the river, delivering the inhabitants into their hand — the marking out of the full extent of the promised land and the assurance of conquest (God fixing the borders of the inheritance: 'I will set thy bounds from the Red sea even unto the sea of the Philistines, and from the desert unto the river: for I will deliver the inhabitants of the land into your hand'; the extent, 'from the Red sea even unto the sea of the Philistines, and from the desert unto the river,' the full compass of the land promised, from the Red Sea to the Mediterranean, from the wilderness to the river Euphrates, the bounds reached in process of time, in the days of David and Solomon; the assurance, 'I will deliver the inhabitants of the land into your hand; and thou shalt drive them out,' that God would give the peoples into their power and they should dispossess them; the faithfulness of God in fixing and in time fulfilling the bounds he promised the fathers; that God both appoints the inheritance and gives the power to possess it).

themes: I will set thy bounds from the Red sea even unto the sea of the Philistines, and from the desert unto the river
for I will deliver the inhabitants of the land into your hand
God marking out the full bounds of the promised land and assuring the conquest
God both appointing the inheritance and giving the power to possess it

schools.summary: Gill: a covenant of peace, a league, a confederacy, so as to take them to be their allies and friends, but they were always to consider them as their enemies; the witnesses note God forbidding to make any covenant with the nations of Canaan or their gods — the charge against all league and fellowship with idolaters (God will have no alliance with the heathen or their idols: 'Thou shalt make no covenant with them, nor with their gods'; the prohibition, 'make no covenant with them,' that Israel should make no league or confederacy with the Canaanites, take them not for allies and friends but ever consider them as their enemies, appointed to destruction; the further charge, 'nor with their gods,' that they should have no fellowship at all with the idols, no truce or tolerance of their worship; the danger guarded against, that a league with the nations would lead to a league with their gods, and so to idolatry, the snare into which Israel afterward fell; the great principle the commentators draw, that God's people must keep wholly separate from the world and its false worship, making no peace with sin or idolatry; that friendship with the enemies of God is enmity with God).

themes: Thou shalt make no covenant with them
nor with their gods
God forbidding any league with the nations of Canaan or their gods
a league with the nations leads to idolatry, friendship with God's enemies enmity with God

schools.summary: Gill: the land of Canaan, given by God for an inheritance, was now to be in the possession of the Israelites, who were not to suffer the old inhabitants to dwell with them; the witnesses note God charging that the nations should not dwell in the land, lest they make Israel sin and their gods become a snare — the reason for the separation, the danger of the idolaters' nearness (God would have the land cleared, lest it become a snare: 'They shall not dwell in thy land, lest they make thee sin against me: for if thou serve their gods, it will surely be a snare unto thee'; the command, 'They shall not dwell in thy land,' that the old inhabitants of Canaan, given to Israel for an inheritance, should not be suffered to remain and dwell among them; the reason, 'lest they make thee sin against me,' that the presence of the idolaters would tempt and draw Israel into their sins, chiefly idolatry; the solemn warning, 'if thou serve their gods, it will surely be a snare unto thee,' that to serve the gods of the heathen would prove a deadly snare, entangling and ruining them, as it afterward did; the great wisdom and mercy of God in the charge, foreseeing the danger and warning against it; the lesson the commentators draw, that the nearness and toleration of evil is a snare, and that God's people must put away every temptation to sin).

themes: They shall not dwell in thy land, lest they make thee sin against me
for if thou serve their gods, it will surely be a snare unto thee
God charging that the nations not dwell in the land, lest they tempt Israel into idolatry
the nearness and toleration of evil a snare, every temptation to sin to be put away
